turns.content: Hey everyone, I'm preparing for an embedded interview and my interviewer mentioned defmt logging. Can someone explain what it is and why it's better than println?!

defmt is a zero-overhead logging framework designed specifically for resource-constrained microcontrollers where you can't afford the overhead of standard formatting libraries like format! or serde_json. Instead of converting types to strings, defmt encodes your data directly into a binary wire format that machines can decode extremely fast without any runtime cost to the application logic itself.
That sounds cool but how do I actually use it in my code? Do I need to add any dependencies or change existing println calls?

You don't need extra crates beyond the defmt crate itself, and you can replace your println! macros with defmt::info! or defmt::error! macros. For example, instead of printing a Temperature struct which might cause heap allocation and string parsing, you would just call defmt::info!(Temperature { value: 23.5 }) and the compiler will generate code that encodes the struct directly into the binary stream.
I see, so there's no heap allocation? What happens when I want to print complex types or just get a raw error message?

You can define your own types that implement the defmt::Display trait and pass them directly into defmt macros. However, if you need custom formatting logic beyond what the type system provides, you might consider using a small helper function wrapped around defmt to handle special cases. The key is keeping everything within the bounds of the Display trait implementation so there's no runtime overhead.
Wait, how do I actually read this binary stream on my desktop? Is there a decoder tool?

Yes! You can use the probe-rs debugger to decode defmt logs in real time while running your firmware. It supports decoding the binary wire format directly from the hardware, so you don't need to manually parse hex dumps or write a separate decoder tool for most use cases.
Great! But what about log levels? Can I differentiate between debug and error messages?

Yes, defmt has built-in macros for different severity levels like defmt::info!, defmt::error!, and defmt::warn!. Each macro corresponds to a specific log level in the binary stream, allowing you to filter messages based on their priority when decoding with probe-rs or other tools.
Perfect! So if I had println!("Error: {}", err) where err is a custom error type, how would that translate?

You'd replace that with defmt::error!(err), and since err implements the Display trait for defmt, it will be encoded as part of the binary stream without any string formatting overhead. The analogy here is like sending a structured packet directly over a wire instead of converting everything to text first.
Thanks! This makes so much more sense now. One last question though, does this work with no_std environments?

Absolutely, defmt is designed to be fully compatible with no_std environments since it doesn't rely on standard library features like fmt or panic hooks that require full std. It's ideal for microcontrollers where you're already in a no_std context anyway.
I'm trying to add logging to my embedded firmware but I thought the standard library had built-in macros for this, so I just wrote println! statements and it broke compilation because there's no std.

That is a very common mistake when switching from desktop Rust to embedded. The standard println! macro relies on the full standard library which is unavailable in no_std environments, so you cannot use it directly. Instead, for zero-overhead logging without the overhead of string formatting or heap allocation, you should implement defmt as your logging framework since it compiles down to a simple wire format.
I see, but I'm confused about how defmt actually works. Isn't it like printf where you pass arguments and it formats them into a string before sending?

Not at all. Think of defmt not as a formatter that builds strings, but rather as a tagged union system similar to how the Rust type system handles enums. When you call defmt::info!("Hello"); it does not create a string; instead it encodes the message into a compact binary wire format on the stack. This is why there is zero runtime overhead compared to println!.
Okay, so I just need to import defmt and use those macros?

You do need to import the crate, but you also need to ensure your trait implementations are correct. The core idea is that every macro call like defmt::error!("Failed"); generates a unique value type at compile time. If you try to use println! in no_std without the right dependencies, it will fail because there is no formatting logic available.
That makes sense, but I'm still worried about how my data gets sent over UART since the wire format looks weird to me.

The binary wire format is what probe-rs uses to decode your logs. When you send defmt output over a serial port, it's not ASCII text; it's a sequence of tagged values that can be decoded by tools like the probe-rs decoder or the Rust Log Parser. This allows you to filter messages based on log levels without parsing strings at runtime.
I don't understand how I can actually see the logs coming out of my UART without some external tool?

You cannot read defmt output directly with a standard terminal because it is not human-readable text. To view your logs, you need to use a decoder like probe-rs or the Rust Log Parser which translates that binary wire format back into readable strings on your host machine.
Wait, so if I just want to see what happened in real-time during boot, I can't do that?

You cannot decode the logs in real-time on the target because you lack the decoder logic. However, you can configure your UART baud rate and buffer size so that when you connect a probe-rs decoder to your host via the same serial port, it will stream the decoded messages back to you as they are sent.
I feel like I'm missing something big here. Why is this so hard compared to just using println?

It feels harder because you have to think in terms of type tags rather than string formatting, but once you get past the initial hurdle of setting up the trait implementations and decoding pipeline, defmt becomes much more efficient. The key difference is that println! requires heap allocation and parsing, while defmt just pushes tagged values onto a stack.

I'm stuck on this exercise where I'm trying to implement a simple sensor node in no_std. I've set up the logging with defmt::Logger but when I run the probe-rs decoder, my messages are coming through as garbage bytes instead of readable strings like 'temp=25'. It seems like my log calls aren't matching what the decoder expects.

The issue is likely that your code isn't actually using defmt logging macros. In no_std environments, you need to ensure you're calling defmt::Logger::info! or error! correctly so the binary wire format gets emitted. If your code just prints strings with println!, those won't be in the defmt format at all and will look like random hex when decoded.

Ah, I was definitely using println! for testing before. But even after switching to defmt::info!("test"), I'm still seeing garbage. Is there something wrong with how the log level is being set or the Logger instance itself?

You need to ensure your code compiles without warnings about unused defmt::Logger. If you're not actually calling any logging macros, the compiler might be optimizing away the setup code, leaving you with an empty or malformed log stream. Check that you have at least one defmt::info! call in hot path and verify your build flags aren't stripping out the logging section.

I added a defmt::info!("hello"); at startup and it's compiling cleanly. But when I decode with probe-rs, I'm still getting these weird byte sequences instead of 'hello'. Is there something about the binary wire format that I'm missing?

The defmt binary wire format is quite specific and doesn't include log level prefixes in the raw bytes. When you call defmt::info!("hello") or error!("error"), those strings are serialized directly into the binary stream without any extra markers. The decoder only knows how to interpret the data based on the function signature, not the macro used.
That makes sense about no prefixes in the wire format! But wait, I tried using defmt::println!("msg") and it worked perfectly. Why would println! work but info! not?

You probably didn't actually use defmt::println! in your code - that's a standard library function, not the defmt macro. The real key is ensuring your logging calls are using the defmt macros and that you're compiling with the correct features enabled for the log level trait.
Wait, I just realized something! My Cargo.toml has defmt = 0.3 but I never added the feature flag for the logging support. That's why my info! calls weren't actually doing anything meaningful!

Exactly! You need to add the feature flag in your Cargo.toml like defmt = { version = "0.3", features = ["log"] } so that the log level trait gets implemented and your macros actually emit data instead of being no-ops.
